Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Data availability lists PYK10/BGLU23/At3g09260 with UniProt Q9SR37.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly given throughout.

Applied_Species.reason: BGLU23 was studied in Arabidopsis and heterologously expressed in onion and tobacco.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Methods/results explicitly mention Arabidopsis thaliana, Allium cepa, and Nicotiana tabacum.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 and Nicotiana tabacum cv. Bright Yellow 2 are stated, but no variety is stated for onion; the combined field as written is only partially supported.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results state woodlice avoid toxic compounds isothiocyanates produced from glucosinolates by BGLU23 and BGLU21.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Hydrolysis products of glucosinolates, including isothiocyanates, are explicitly discussed as defense compounds.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The Results directly link BGLU23/BGLU21 to chemical defense against woodlice.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper states the defense involves ER-body glucosidases and vacuolar substrates in seedlings and roots upon tissue damage.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: BGLU23 is placed in glucosinolate hydrolysis / mustard-oil bomb-related defense in the paper.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The study explicitly describes a single-cell ER body-vacuole defense system in seedlings and roots.

Enzyme_Name.reason: BGLU23 is described as a β-glucosidase and also called PYK10.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: BGLU23 is described as a β-glucosidase with a C-terminal ER-retention signal KDEL.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results support hydrolysis of endogenous glucosinolates and inference to toxic compounds including isothiocyanates upon damage.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Metabolomics identified 13 endogenous glucosinolates across aliphatic, aromatic, and indole classes as substrates in the double-mutant comparison.

Primary_Product.reason: Toxic compounds including isothiocyanates are inferred/discussed, but the hydrolysis products were not directly measured in vivo in Results.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The paper's central model is that BGLU23 converts stored glucosinolates into toxic defense products upon organellar damage.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: BGLU23 is presented as the glucosinolate-converting enzyme producing toxic compounds, i.e. a terminal defensive conversion step.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports BGLU23 as a key determinant of defense, but does not test rate limitation.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: The paper states BGLU23 is abundant in seedlings and roots, major component of ER bodies, while TGG1/TGG2 are undetectable in seedlings.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results show BGLU23 accumulates in Arabidopsis ER bodies/root hair cells and localizes to induced ER bodies in onion and tobacco with NAI2.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: All listed contexts are in the paper: seedling/root stages, tissue damage/feeding, 26°C 30 min incubation, scopoletin treatment in tobacco, and coexpression with NAI2.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state Pro35S constructs for BGLU23/BGLU23-GFP in onion/tobacco; native NAI2 promoter was used for NAI2-GFP lines.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: BGLU23 localizes to ER bodies/ER lumen and moves to vacuoles when NAI2 is absent.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: NAI2 interaction is shown in this paper; PYK10-binding protein 1 is mentioned as prior work in the Introduction.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are directly used in Results for BGLU23.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The double mutant retained glucosinolates after incubation and was more susceptible to woodlice, supporting this summary.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All five points are directly supported by Results, except that substrate hydrolysis is shown jointly with BGLU21.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show vacuolar leakage and reduced BGLU23 abundance without NAI2; Discussion proposes premature reaction with vacuolar glucosides as a risk.

Omics_Data.reason: Data availability lists MetaboLights MTBLS1383 and PRIDE PXD016606.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper explicitly suggests NAI2 and BGLU23 can induce ER bodies in non-Brassicaceae plants, supporting engineering relevance.

Other_Important_Info.reason: These statements match Table 2, the KDEL deletion experiment, and the fact that in vivo products were not comprehensively measured.

corrections.reason: No EC number is provided in the paper.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Data availability lists BGLU21/At1g66270 with UniProt Q9C525.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results state toxic compounds isothiocyanates are produced from glucosinolates by BGLU23 and BGLU21.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Glucosinolate hydrolysis products including isothiocyanates are discussed.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The double mutant assay links BGLU21 together with BGLU23 to defense against woodlice.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper discusses glucosinolates in vacuoles and defense in seedlings/roots generally, but does not specifically assign this site to BGLU21.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: BGLU21 is described as an ER-body β-glucosidase acting on glucosinolates in this defense system.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: It is part of the single-cell ER body-vacuole defense system described in the paper.

Enzyme_Name.reason: BGLU21 is explicitly named and described as a β-glucosidase.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is stated for BGLU21.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper describes BGLU21 as a β-glucosidase localized to ER bodies and notes BGLU23 and BGLU21 both have KDEL signals.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The double mutant metabolomics supports BGLU21 acting with BGLU23 to hydrolyze endogenous glucosinolates after damage/homogenization.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Various endogenous glucosinolates are supported from the double-mutant metabolomics.

Primary_Product.reason: Toxic products including isothiocyanates are inferred, but no direct in vivo product measurement is shown for BGLU21.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The paper links BGLU21 with BGLU23 to conversion of stored glucosinolates into toxic defense products.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: As a glucosinolate-converting β-glucosidase, it functions at the terminal defensive conversion step.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The wording says contributory terminal enzyme and less abundant than BGLU23; the paper explicitly states BGLU21 is less abundant.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results say BGLU21 is a less abundant β-glucosidase of ER bodies.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Its functional presence in Arabidopsis seedlings is shown via the bglu23 bglu21 mutant analysis.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Seedling stage, feeding assay, and homogenate incubation at 26°C for 30 min are all in Results.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper states A. thaliana's closest BGLU23 homolog, BGLU21, is also localized to ER bodies.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The listed double-mutant metabolomics and feeding assay were used.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The bglu23 bglu21 mutant retained glucosinolates and showed greater susceptibility to woodlice.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three statements are supported, though the contribution is not separated from BGLU23.

Omics_Data.reason: Metabolomics deposition MTBLS1383 is in Data availability.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not discuss BGLU21 as an engineering stacking partner.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper indeed does not separate BGLU21's individual contribution and describes it as less abundant than BGLU23.

corrections.reason: No EC number is stated for BGLU21.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Data availability lists NAI2/At3g15950 with UniProt Q9LSB4.

Applied_Species.reason: NAI2 was studied in Arabidopsis and heterologously expressed in onion and tobacco.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana, Allium cepa, and Nicotiana tabacum are explicitly named.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 and BY-2 are stated, but onion variety is not given; the combined field is only partly supported.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: NAI2 is essential for ER-body formation and defense leading to toxic compounds from glucosinolates, but NAI2 does not itself directly produce or measure these metabolites.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Hydrolysis products including isothiocyanates are part of the defense model, but not directly assigned/measured for NAI2 in Results.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: NAI2 deficiency causes loss of resistance to woodlice, supporting a role in chemical defense against herbivory.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper discusses this defense system in seedlings and roots, with vacuolar glucosinolates and ER-body BGLU23 spatial separation until damage.

Regulator_Type.reason: Results show NAI2 is required for ER-body formation and BGLU23 sequestration, fitting organelle biogenesis/sequestration factor.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results describe NAI2 as an ER-body component with ten repeats of ~40-aa sequence containing acidic motif Glu-Phe-Glu and as Brassicaceae-specific.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: NAI2 is clearly an ER-body component, but the paper does not explicitly state 'luminal component' in the wording of this field.

Protein_Complex_Involvement.reason: Co-immunoprecipitation and proteomics support interaction/association with BGLU23 in an ER-body-related complex/assembly.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: BGLU23 is strongly supported as an interacting protein.

Regulation_Mode.reason: Results show NAI2 controls ER-body formation and prevents BGLU23 leakage/degradation by sequestration.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: The paper directly supports effects on BGLU23 localization/accumulation and ER-body formation.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: The paper supports effects on BGLU23 protein localization and accumulation, but not on target genes per se; wording with 'genes' is imprecise.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: NAI2 controls compartmentation of glucosinolate-hydrolyzing BGLU23 in the single-cell defense system.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: Results support NAI2 as essential for ER-body formation, BGLU23 maintenance, and defense based on glucosinolate hydrolysis.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Native promoter-driven NAI2-GFP expression was examined in Arabidopsis seedlings, and NAI2 is described as an ER-body component in Brassicaceae.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results show that expression with BGLU23 induces ER bodies in onion and tobacco, and nai2 mutants lack ER bodies in Arabidopsis.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: All listed contexts are present in the paper: seedlings/roots, heterologous expression, and woodlouse feeding assays.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state Pro35S for NAI2 in onion/tobacco and native NAI2 promoter for NAI2-GFP in Arabidopsis.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods were used for NAI2 in Results.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show NAI2 enables ER-body formation, stabilizes/sequesters BGLU23, and is required for full defense against woodlice.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four statements are directly supported by Results.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Loss of NAI2 abolishes ER bodies and reduces BGLU23 due to apparent degradation; no growth penalty is reported.

Omics_Data.reason: Proteomics dataset PXD016606 is listed in Data availability.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper explicitly argues that acquisition/expression of NAI2 with BGLU23 can reconstruct ER bodies in non-Brassicaceae plants.

Other_Important_Info.reason: These statements are consistent with the paper's mechanistic conclusions and evolutionary proposal.
